content.text: Every call to a language model involves a fundamental constraint: the model can, in principle, generate tokens indefinitely. Two parameters — max_tokens and stop sequences — impose clean termination boundaries. Understanding how they interact, where they fail, and how to size them correctly is essential for building reliable, cost-controlled AI systems.
What it is

max_tokens (called max_completion_tokens in some OpenAI endpoints, or simply max_tokens in the Anthropic API) is an integer that caps the number of tokens the model may produce in a single forward pass. When the model has emitted that many tokens, the API halts generation and returns whatever has been built so far, regardless of whether the output is semantically complete. The corresponding finish_reason field in the response is set to "length".

Stop sequences (called stop in OpenAI and stop_sequences in Anthropic) are one or more literal strings that, when the model emits them verbatim, immediately halt generation. The API strips the matched delimiter from the response and sets finish_reason to "stop". Unlike max_tokens, stop sequences encode semantic meaning — they mark boundaries between structured sections, turns in a conversation, or phases in a multi-step pipeline.

The two controls are orthogonal but complementary. max_tokens provides an absolute safety cap on cost and latency. Stop sequences provide a semantic stopping criterion that fires early when the model emits an agreed-upon marker. In production agentic systems, both are almost always set simultaneously: max_tokens as the hard budget, stop sequences as the expected completion signal.
Why it matters

Without output caps, a single misconfigured prompt can generate tens of thousands of tokens before the API enforces its own hard limits. At typical token pricing this translates directly to unexpected costs. More critically, in agent pipelines where models call tools in loops, a model that never stops creates a runaway cycle: the orchestrator waits, downstream steps stall, and the overall pipeline either times out or exhausts its budget.

Stop sequences solve a subtler problem: structured output. When a model is prompted to emit a response in phases — reason first, then answer — or to produce structured sections separated by XML-like tags, the orchestrator needs a way to extract each phase without post-processing the entire response. A stop sequence of "</reasoning>" tells the model exactly where one phase ends, and the caller can then collect that chunk and issue a continuation prompt to extract the next phase.

In agentic AI, these parameters also gate tool calling. Many agent frameworks instruct the model to emit a structured tool call in a specific format, then halt with a stop sequence (e.g., "]\n" or "</tool_call>"). The orchestrator parses the tool call, executes it, and injects the result before continuing. If max_tokens is too small, the tool call JSON may arrive truncated and be unparseable; without a stop sequence, the model may continue beyond the tool call boundary into hallucinated responses.
Benefits
Cost control:
Setting max_tokens close to the expected output size prevents runaway generation and ties per-call cost to a predictable upper bound.
Latency predictability:
A hard token cap translates to a maximum time-to-completion, enabling SLA guarantees for latency-sensitive pipelines.
Structured output parsing:
Stop sequences allow callers to extract one semantically complete section at a time without running a full response through a parser.
Runaway prevention:
In agent loops, a finite max_tokens budget ensures that any single step terminates, even if the model falls into a repetitive or verbose completion.
Explicit truncation signaling:
finish_reason == "length" is an unambiguous signal that the model was budget-limited, allowing callers to implement continuation logic rather than silently accepting incomplete output.
Trade-offs
Truncation risk:
A max_tokens value set too low cuts output mid-word, mid-JSON object, or mid-code block. For structured formats, this produces an unparseable string.
False stop triggers:
Stop sequences can appear inside legitimate content. A stop string of "User:" will halt generation if the model quotes a conversation transcript.
Reasoning model overhead:
Extended-thinking models consume tokens for internal reasoning before visible output. If max_tokens does not account for the thinking budget, the model may not produce any visible answer.
Stop sequence stripping:
The matched delimiter is removed from the response. If the caller needs to know which stop sequence fired, they must infer it from context or from an API-specific field.
No semantic guarantee:
max_tokens does not guarantee that the model will produce a meaningful, complete response. It only guarantees that generation stops by a certain point.
How it works

LLM inference is an autoregressive process: the model samples one token at a time, appends it to the context, and repeats until a stopping condition fires. Each forward pass through the transformer produces a probability distribution over the vocabulary, and one token is selected via greedy decoding, sampling, or beam search. This process is entirely controlled by the serving layer — the model weights themselves have no inherent concept of "done".
max_tokens is enforced by the serving layer with a simple counter. Let n be the number of tokens generated so far. Generation continues while:

When n reaches max_tokens the serving layer stops the loop and returns the accumulated tokens. The model's own end-of-sequence token (EOS) can also stop generation before this limit — finish_reason == "stop" without any explicit stop_sequences set means the model emitted its natural EOS token, which counts as a clean completion.

Stop sequences are checked against the output buffer after each token is appended. The check is a suffix match: if the concatenated output ends with any of the listed stop strings, generation halts immediately. For multi-token stop sequences, the implementation maintains a rolling window over the last k characters (where k is the length of the longest stop string). This means a stop sequence like "</answer>" fires correctly even if the model emits it across multiple short tokens.
The total cost of a single generation call is the sum of prompt tokens and completion tokens. max_tokens bounds the completion side:
Where p_in and p_out are the per-token input and output prices. Setting max_tokens tight minimizes expected cost without sacrificing completeness for normal inputs.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Token generation loop with max_tokens counter and stop sequence checker

content.text: The diagram shows the two stopping conditions operating in parallel on each decoding step. After each token is appended to the output buffer, the serving layer checks both conditions: whether the token budget is exhausted (finish_reason = "length") and whether the buffer ends with a registered stop sequence (finish_reason = "stop"). When neither condition is met, control loops back to the LLM decoder for the next token. The caller must inspect finish_reason to distinguish a natural stop from a truncated output.
When to use it

max_tokens should be set on every production API call without exception. The only decision is the value. Stop sequences are situational — they add value when output has structural phases the caller needs to parse incrementally, or when the model is prompted to emit explicit delimiters.
Set max_tokens when
The output format is known: derive the cap from the worst-case token count of a valid output plus a 20-30% margin.
Operating in an agent loop where any single step must terminate within a bounded time budget.
The system must enforce cost-per-call limits for billing or rate-limit compliance.
Calling reasoning models that have a separate thinking budget drawn from the same max_tokens pool.
Use stop sequences when
Prompting the model for multi-phase output: halt after each phase with a stop sequence, collect the chunk, and issue a continuation prompt.
Building tool-use pipelines where the model emits a structured tool call followed by a delimiter.
Parsing XML-tagged reasoning traces: stop on "</reasoning>" to isolate the reasoning block, then continue to extract the answer.
Avoid stop sequences when
The delimiter could appear naturally in the output (prose responses, code that contains the delimiter string).
Using native tool-calling APIs that already manage turn boundaries through the message structure.
How to implement
Step 1: Estimate the output token budget

Before setting max_tokens, compute the worst-case token count of a valid output. For structured outputs, render the full schema with maximum-length field values and tokenize it. Add a 25% margin for preamble tokens the model may emit before the structured content.

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")

# Worst-case output for a product summary schema
worst_case = (
    '{"name": "' + "A" * 100 + '",'
    ' "description": "' + "B" * 500 + '",'
    ' "tags": [' + ", ".join(['"C"'] * 10) + "]}"
)
token_count = len(enc.encode(worst_case))

# Add 25% margin for model preamble
max_tokens = int(token_count * 1.25)
print(f"Worst-case tokens: {token_count}, recommended max_tokens: {max_tokens}")
Step 2: Make the API call with both parameters
Always pass max_tokens and, when using structured output with phases, stop_sequences. Handle stop_reason explicitly in the response.
Step 3: Handle truncation with continuation
When finish_reason is "length", the output is incomplete. For structured formats, retry with a higher max_tokens. For free-form prose, a continuation prompt can extend the output.

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

def generate_with_continuation(
    prompt: str,
    max_tokens: int = 512,
    max_retries: int = 3,
) -> str:
    """Generate text, continuing automatically if truncated."""
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    full_text = ""

    for _ in range(max_retries):
        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o-mini",
            messages=messages,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
        )
        chunk = response.choices[0].message.content
        finish = response.choices[0].finish_reason
        full_text += chunk

        if finish == "stop":
            break  # Clean completion
        elif finish == "length":
            # Append partial response and ask to continue
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": chunk})
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": "Continue exactly from where you left off."})
        else:
            break

    return full_text

result = generate_with_continuation(
    "Write a detailed explanation of how a B-tree index works.",
    max_tokens=300,
)
print(result)
Examples
Example 1: Basic max_tokens and stop sequence
The simplest use case: cap a completion and use a stop sequence to end at a natural boundary.

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "Answer concisely. End with </answer>."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is an embedding vector?"},
    ],
    max_tokens=150,
    stop=["</answer>"],
)

print("Text:  ", response.choices[0].message.content)
print("Finish:", response.choices[0].finish_reason)  # "stop" or "length"
Example 2: Multi-phase structured output with stop sequences
Extract reasoning and final answer as separate strings by using stop sequences to halt after each phase and issuing a continuation prompt.

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

def extract_phases(problem: str) -> dict:
    """Prompt the model to reason then answer; extract each as a separate string."""
    base_messages = [{
        "role": "user",
        "content": (
            f"Solve this step by step.\n"
            f"Wrap your reasoning in <reasoning>...</reasoning>.\n"
            f"Then give your final answer in <answer>...</answer>.\n"
            f"Problem: {problem}"
        )
    }]

    # Phase 1: extract reasoning block
    r1 = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
        max_tokens=600,
        stop_sequences=["</reasoning>"],
        messages=base_messages,
    )
    reasoning = r1.content[0].text.removeprefix("<reasoning>").strip()

    # Phase 2: continue from after </reasoning> to get the answer
    r2 = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
        max_tokens=200,
        stop_sequences=["</answer>"],
        messages=base_messages + [
            {
                "role": "assistant",
                "content": f"<reasoning>\n{reasoning}\n</reasoning>\n<answer>"
            }
        ],
    )
    answer = r2.content[0].text.strip()

    return {"reasoning": reasoning, "answer": answer}

result = extract_phases(
    "A train travels 120 km in 1.5 hours. What is its average speed in m/s?"
)
print("Reasoning:", result["reasoning"][:80], "...")
print("Answer:   ", result["answer"])
Real-world example

A production document intelligence pipeline receives PDF contracts and extracts structured metadata (title, date, parties, key clauses) as JSON. Each document is processed by a GPT-4o-mini call with the full document text in context.

The engineering team ran 10,000 sample documents through a staging pipeline and found that output JSON ranged from 180 to 420 tokens. They set max_tokens to 600 (420 * 1.43x margin) and added a stop sequence of "```" to catch cases where the model wraps JSON in a code fence, which would corrupt the downstream json.loads() call.

import json
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

SYSTEM_PROMPT = (
    "You are a document analysis assistant. Extract the following fields from the document "
    "and return them as a raw JSON object with no surrounding text or markdown. "
    "Fields: title, date, parties (list), summary."
)

def extract_document_metadata(document_text: str) -> dict:
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
            {"role": "user", "content": f"Document:\n{document_text}"},
        ],
        max_tokens=600,
        stop=["```"],       # Halt if model starts a code fence
        temperature=0,      # Deterministic output
        response_format={"type": "json_object"},
    )

    finish = response.choices[0].finish_reason
    raw = response.choices[0].message.content

    if finish == "length":
        raise ValueError(
            f"Output truncated at {response.usage.completion_tokens} tokens. "
            "Raise max_tokens or reduce document chunk size."
        )

    return json.loads(raw)

This pattern — sample worst-case output sizes, add margin, set max_tokens, add defensive stop sequences, and treat finish_reason == "length" as a hard error — is the standard production approach for structured extraction pipelines.
Common mistakes
Mistake 1: Not setting max_tokens at all

The default max_tokens for most APIs is a large number (often the full context window minus prompt tokens). Relying on the default exposes the system to runaway generation and unpredictable cost spikes.

# WRONG: no max_tokens set — model may generate thousands of tokens
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write an essay about climate change."}],
)

# RIGHT: set a meaningful cap based on expected output size
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a 3-sentence summary of climate change causes."}],
    max_tokens=150,
)
Mistake 2: Setting max_tokens too low for structured output

A max_tokens value that truncates a JSON object mid-field produces a string that json.loads() will reject. The only recovery is a full retry — more expensive than simply setting a sufficient cap from the start.
Mistake 3: Using a stop sequence that appears in the content

# WRONG: "Note:" is common in model output — will cause premature stops
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize the history of the internet."}],
    stop=["Note:"],
)

# RIGHT: use a syntactically unambiguous XML-like delimiter
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{
        "role": "user",
        "content": "Summarize the history of the internet. End your summary with </summary>."
    }],
    stop=["</summary>"],
)
Mistake 4: Ignoring finish_reason

# WRONG: treat response as complete without checking finish_reason
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Return a JSON object with 20 fields."}],
    max_tokens=200,
)
data = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)  # May raise — or silently corrupt

# RIGHT: always check before processing
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Return a JSON object with 20 fields."}],
    max_tokens=200,
)
choice = response.choices[0]
if choice.finish_reason == "length":
    raise ValueError("LLM output was truncated — increase max_tokens or reduce schema size.")
data = json.loads(choice.message.content)
Best practices
Always set max_tokens on every production API call.
There is no valid use case for leaving it unset in a production environment.
Derive max_tokens from tokenized sample outputs, not intuition.
Run a tokenizer over 50-100 representative outputs, take the 95th percentile, add a 20-30% margin, and use that as your cap.
Treat finish_reason == "length" as a hard error in structured output pipelines.
A truncated JSON or tool call is worse than no output — raise an exception, log it, and retry rather than passing partial content downstream.
Choose stop sequences that are syntactically unambiguous.
Prefer XML-like closing tags ("</section>") or UUID-based delimiters over natural-language phrases that may appear spontaneously in model output.
Account for reasoning token budgets separately.
For reasoning models, the thinking budget comes from the same pool as visible output tokens. Set max_tokens high enough that thinking does not consume all available space.
Monitor token utilization in production.
Track completion_tokens / max_tokens ratios. A ratio consistently near 1.0 means the cap is too tight and silent truncation is occurring.
Version stop sequences with prompt changes.
Stop sequences are part of the prompt contract. If the delimiter changes in the prompt, update the API call parameter atomically.
Performance & scalability

Token generation is autoregressive: each forward pass costs roughly the same compute regardless of how many tokens have already been generated (for a fixed KV-cache size). Time to generate n tokens scales approximately linearly:

max_tokens therefore directly controls the maximum generation latency for any single request. For latency-sensitive applications, keep max_tokens tight and use streaming to deliver partial responses to the user while generation continues in the background.

At scale, max_tokens interacts with batching efficiency. LLM serving systems group requests into batches and pad shorter sequences to match the longest one. A high max_tokens value forces the serving layer to reserve KV-cache memory for the full potential output length even if the model stops early. This reduces throughput — more memory per request means fewer concurrent requests. Keeping max_tokens close to actual expected output length improves serving utilization.

Stop sequences improve throughput by enabling early exit. If 80% of requests complete in under 100 tokens, a stop sequence that fires at natural completion frees batch slots earlier than waiting for max_tokens. For high-throughput pipelines, always pair a stop sequence with the token cap — the stop sequence handles the common case efficiently, and max_tokens handles the pathological case safely.
Streaming considerations

When using the streaming API, max_tokens still applies — the stream ends when the budget is exhausted. The finish_reason appears in the final streamed chunk. Stop sequences also work in streaming mode, but the matched delimiter may appear in an earlier chunk before the stream terminates. Always buffer the full stream and check the terminal finish_reason before treating the content as complete.
Tooling & ecosystem
All major LLM SDKs expose max_tokens and stop sequences, but parameter names and finish_reason values differ across providers.
OpenAI Python SDK:
max_tokens or max_completion_tokens (newer models), stop (list, max 4 strings). finish_reason in choices[0].finish_reason. Values: "stop", "length", "tool_calls", "content_filter".
Anthropic Python SDK:
max_tokens (required), stop_sequences (list). stop_reason in response.stop_reason. Values: "end_turn", "max_tokens", "stop_sequence", "tool_use".
Google Vertex AI / Gemini:
max_output_tokens in GenerationConfig, stop_sequences list. finish_reason in candidates[0].finish_reason.
LangChain / LangGraph:
Pass max_tokens= and stop= as kwargs to ChatOpenAI / ChatAnthropic. Access finish_reason via the response's response_metadata dict.
tiktoken (OpenAI):
Use to count tokens before the call to size max_tokens accurately without making an API request.
Anthropic token counting:
client.messages.count_tokens() returns the token count for a given message list without generating a response.

# Count tokens before calling the API (Anthropic)
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

count = client.messages.count_tokens(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain gradient descent in detail."}],
)
print(f"Prompt tokens: {count.input_tokens}")
# Use this to set max_tokens relative to the model's context window limit

Several other mechanisms can limit or structure LLM output. The comparison below covers the most common alternatives.

Security & reliability

max_tokens is the primary defense against prompt injection attacks that attempt to generate runaway output. An attacker who injects content designed to make the model produce very long responses — padding with filler, recursive lists, or repetition loops — is bounded by the configured max_tokens value. Without it, a single injected prompt could exhaust rate limits or billing quotas.

Stop sequences introduce a reliability risk: an adversarial input that causes the model to emit the stop sequence unexpectedly, truncating the legitimate output. If the stop sequence is "STOP" and the model is summarizing user-submitted text containing that word, generation halts prematurely. Mitigations include choosing stop sequences that are syntactically distinct from user content (UUID-based delimiters, namespace-prefixed tags) and asserting that the stop occurred at the expected position in the response.

In multi-tenant systems, per-tenant max_tokens limits should be enforced at the API gateway layer — not solely in application code. A misconfigured client or a client that bypasses the SDK can still make raw API calls. Rate limiting by output tokens (not just request count) provides stronger cost-per-tenant isolation.
Reliability checklist
Always check finish_reason before parsing structured output.
Log finish_reason distributions in production — a spike in "length" indicates the token budget is too tight.
Use unique, non-natural-language stop sequences for security-sensitive pipelines.
Enforce per-tenant max_tokens limits at the gateway layer in multi-tenant systems.
For reasoning models, monitor thinking token consumption separately to ensure visible output budget is adequate.
Include max_tokens in integration tests: deliberately set a low cap and assert that finish_reason == "length" is detected and handled.
Key takeaways
max_tokens is a hard budget on generated tokens; set it on every production API call to prevent runaway cost and stalled pipelines.
Stop sequences are semantic delimiters: they halt generation when the model emits a chosen string and enable structured multi-phase output extraction.
finish_reason ("stop" vs "length" vs "end_turn") is the reliable signal for distinguishing clean completion from budget-truncated output — always inspect it.
Size max_tokens by tokenizing worst-case valid outputs and adding a 20-30% margin; avoid round numbers chosen by intuition.
Choose stop sequences that are syntactically unambiguous and unlikely to appear spontaneously in model output — prefer XML-like closing tags over natural-language phrases.
Treat finish_reason == "length" as a hard error in structured output pipelines; truncated JSON or tool-call syntax produces silent corruption downstream.
For high-throughput serving, tight max_tokens values combined with stop sequences improve batch efficiency by releasing KV-cache memory on early completion.
Reasoning models require extra accounting: the thinking token budget and the visible output token budget both draw from the same max_tokens pool on most APIs.
